item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this He-Who-Made mythos, and I think the core of this article is strong as well, but there are a few things keeping me in no-vote country at the moment.

The redactions are a big one.
– hide block

All instances of SCP-1736-1 and SCP-1736-2 share a common religion that bears strong similarities to [REDACTED].

Alright, fine. It's a religion, don't want to specify, but we can choose whichever one we think it feels like. This is the only good redaction in this article.

This deity is most often referred to as [REDACTED] or [REDACTED].

This entire sentence is completely meaningless. Show us what he's called, or remove this sentence. It's distracting as is.

When SCP-1736-1 does this, SCP-1736-1 emits dangerously high levels of ionizing radiation, measured as high as [REDACTED] death within 48 to 72 hours for a typical human subject.

This one's a little more fidgety. I don't see any reason to bother redacting the amounts/types of radiation unless you're trying to avoid the science details in order to avoid losing opinion based on screwing up the science. That part's fine. But redacting random chunks in the middle of sentences pisses me off to no end, and there's a LOT of sentence missing there besides how much radiation -1 lets off. When you leave that much out of the sentence, there's no way for a reader to 'fill in' partial detail, because it's too open-ended. I could tell an entire unrelated story in that redaction.

using language such as, “Come, He brings enlightenment,” “He is here to lead us away from the darkness,” and [REDACTED].

Similar to the previous one. There's too little context to really pick out exactly what he's likely to say, or WHY they would redact one phrase and not the rest. This one would be fine just removing that redaction and leaving the rest.

There's some minor tone oddity that others mentioned, but it didn't really bother me much.

I LOVED the Inspector General investigation, and I kind of wish there was a little more coherence in that portion - he seems to have a really fascinating thread of investigation that seems really scattered to us, but it's such a great inclusion. I'm not mad at the way it's currently set up, but given the fairly original idea being added into the article, it could maybe be strengthened to pick this article up more on the basis of the investigation as opposed to the sparse information given on the radioactive cult, which despite the awesome mythos is a bit less original/notable(Children of the Atom much?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is just one thing missing from the description of this skip: Explaining how the Foundation discovered SCP-1736 following the fiasco involving Dr. Kennerman and the IG's report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First I read this without following any of the cross-links (I had read them all before, but had forgotten them). I was spectacularly unimpressed.

So then I read it again, following all the links. It look me a damned long time, but the payoff was very good. I like world-building, and though this is done slowly and a bit awkwardly (because it requires so much jumping around and reading), ultimately i thought it was worth it. I'm still not totally fascinated by the He-who-made mythos, but I'm intrigued, at least. I like the duality aspect not being just good vs. evil - it's nice the consideration of light being just as powerful and deadly because of radiation. I've always been very creeped out by radiation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough, if it didn't click for you it didn't click.

I just think that providing a jumping off point for wonky head canon about the Foundation is good and sufficient justification for an item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To echo Lumancer's words below, this is the first skip of this series that didn'tgrab me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember when I decided to move from lurker to member.
I had just finished reading 914. What with clicking every link, then every link from the new page, coming back to the test log and repeating; it took me about 3 days to read that "one" article. It was spectacular and I wanted in.

914 is excellent in its own right, but it was all that cross-linking that really hooked me. It took the Foundation from a themed creepy-pasta collection and made it a collaborative fiction universe, an interconnected setting worth exploring. My greatest reservation about the way we discourage cross-testing so strongly is that it incidentally discourages that world-building I am so fond of.

Should every new article link to every other one, no matter how flimsy the justification? HELL NO! But when a concept strong enough to bear the weight comes along, I say cross-link as you see fit. And I'll thank you for it.

TL;DR:I like crossing scps when done well and the concept supports it. This qualifies easily for both and the article is all the better for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You say that like it's a bad thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the first time that this mythos has managed to grab me. This is a slow read given all of the cross links, but it's an interesting payoff. I may have to revisit the other items in the mythos now…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Footnotes of another skips would be a huge security problem in-universe. Out-universe would make footnotes as an extra article by themselves, and that's not cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1736-1 must be kept restrained at all times, and nutrition and hydration shall be administered intravenously. No lights shall be permitted in SCP-1736-1’s containment chamber,

Must be kind of frustrating for the person who has to stick the needle in while completely blind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beginning: meh, seen religion-of-crazy before.

Addenda: ……

End: neat.

Now, this is how you pull off a big twist. That sudden smash cut to an internal investigation was brilliant.

As a suggestion, one way you could avoid people needing to look at the other SCPs' pages would be to insert summaries in the footnotes, so for example 'SCP-719 is a television set that presents viewers with visions describing a religion related to [REDACTED]'. Snag is those descriptions would always lose a bit compared to the original, unless you were a much better summariser than I am.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only problem with this one is that Im curious, and because of that I must read every linked SCP (again if I already read them) to continue. You are one of the few people who I consider is allowed to do this properly (specially because it all fits in you He-who-made-dark/light canon).

Edit: 4 upvotes… Im not kiddind. I upvoted 4 different articles just reading this one. You sir make me read a lot, but it totally worths it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why all the links to other SCPs? I quite honestly lost track of what was going on by the time I got to the final addendum. It's like the religion part is just a jumping-off point for a bunch of wonky headcanon about the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how every new addition to this series gives me an "oh shit!" feeling. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Disappointed that it wasn't related to corn god man, but then I read it and it was good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A few too many [REDACTED]s for my liking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP 1718. Long story short, it was supposed to be a containment unit, but when it was turned on it turned out… wrong. So the unit itself needs to be contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the "containment chamber?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This actually grew out of the idea of what might happen in a crosstest between 719 and 1127… But I suspect any anomalous broadcast that had a effect on the viewer would have worked as well for the purpouse, providing a conduit for He-Who-Made-Light to jump from the screen into the viewer's mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upon reflection I agree with your disagreement and edited the "skip"s in the final addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aided by the complacency of our staff, the chain of command, and the morass of bureaucracy we've constructed, he was able to act with relative impunity.

I love this sentence. I wish I could have enjoyed the whole setup that lead to this more, but still, this is ingenious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh, fun stuff. Surprised at the link to 1127, but I guess it works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the idea behind this, the execution, and the mythos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno.

The Foundation Investigation was the most interesting part of this, but I don't think it was enough to push it into upvote territory. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really disagree with the colloquial word "skip" being used in an O5 meeting. Other than that I'm ambivalent as hell, though that may be the lack of sleep talking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always like SCPs that provide insight into how the Foundation operates, and this peek into the institutional inertia of a massive bureaucracy is quite nice. And the Foundation isn't perfect by any means; I like that you've shown how they can screw up and end up actually making a new dangerous SCP (like that anti-meme or the "containment chamber"). Unlike some other comments, I feel that the crosslinking is entirely appropriate here, since the use of the other He-Who-Made SCPs is what allowed this researcher to create/resurrect the religion. I do feel that it could use some streamlining, but overall I enjoy it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea that the Foundation created SCP-1736 is interesting, though it feels a little like an excuse to link to other skips. I can't help but feel the core premise - the radioactive religion - could do with some further development, perhaps in the form of examples of the belief system; I was waiting for an interview with the preacher the Foundation has in custody but it never arrived. I do gather it's part of a wider mythos but I agree the piece doesn't quite feel like it stands on its own. No vote from me at the minute, I'm afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tonally this just feels off. For example:

During such an event, approximately 20% of SCP-1736-2 in attendance will attempt to persuade passers-by to come and listen to SCP-1736-1 using language such as, “come, He brings enlightenment,” and “He is here to lead us away from the darkness,” and [REDACTED]

These examples don't really add to the readers understanding of the SCP. It seems more like an attempt to build up this mythos, but isn't really relevant to the SCP itself.

Additionally, in the containment procedures you list off a series of clunky contingency plans. While I see the appeal, for me they just slowed down the read with needless detail. Remember, these articles aren't meant to be exhaustive; a single line saying "In the event of a containment breach, see Document 1736-AJ1 for recontainment procedures" would cover it just fine. Then, if you want to go into exhaustive detail, make another page and link it in.

Besides that, it just doesn't do much for me. I haven't read any of the other relevant material, but that's kind of the point; SCPs need to stand on their own WITHOUT any of the other backstory, and this one doesn't (for me at least). It's a radioactive dude and some radioactive followers. As I was reading it, I kept waiting for the hook/twist, and it never came.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: People are free to downvote for any reason related to the article, no matter how minor. In the case of grammar/spelling errors, many voters with remove or reverse their vote once those errors have been corrected. Also note that editing in order to correct basic grammar & spelling errors is permitted for anyone, so long as the actual content of the article is preserved. I.e. fixing verb tenses is okay but rewriting a sentence isn't. Also note that both U.K. and American spellings are acceptable, so make sure that the spelling error isn't just an alternate spelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The articles are supposed to be finished and professional. There really isn't any room for those kind of mistakes and they definitely kick the reader out of the narrative. Besides, I'm sure if they were fixed the downvote would become a no vote or a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not trying to be mean, but are grammar and spelling issues reasons to downvote? I mean, if it were painfully bad, using some sort of l33t speak or in some horrible black-speech like Hungarian(I jest, I jest, it's not that bad..), yeah, but I think the idea carries it through. Then again, I freely admit I'm a huge fan of the He-who-Made mythos, so I'm mildly biased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually started a line-by-line for this because I saw it already had +3 but had to give up half way through.
There are too many tone / spelling / grammar errors for me to help you with, so I'll leave it to one of the better writers on the site.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My latest contribution to the "He-who-made-dark" mythos. I tried to do something a little different this time, concentrating more on the worshipers than the brothers themselves.

This could be seen as a complement to SCP-1806, inverting it the same way SCP-719 inverts SCP-435.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yesssss. More new stuff for the He-who-made-Light and He-who-made-Dark mythos!

+1
